intro: Moving from JavaScript to R transitions software engineering mindsets from general-purpose web application development to specialized statistical computing and data analysis. Developers adopt R to utilize its vectorization engines, complex mathematical formulas, and robust built-in plotting capabilities. This migration replaces web-centric asynchronous event models with synchronous, data-frame-centric pipelines.

diffs.from: Dynamically typed, relying on prototypes, primitives, and object references.
diffs.to: Dynamically typed, but built entirely around vectors, matrices, factors, and data frames with 1-based indexing.

diffs.from: C-style curly brace syntax using standard assignment (=), functions, and lexical scoping.
diffs.to: Functional, mathematical syntax using arrow assignment (<-), dot-separated or snake_case names, and implicit returns.

diffs.from: Automatic garbage collection optimized for short-lived UI components and event triggers in a browser or V8 runtime.
diffs.to: In-memory architecture where all datasets must fit within RAM, managed by a synchronous copy-on-write garbage collector.

diffs.from: Non-blocking event loop native to the engine, heavily leveraging Promises and callback queues.
diffs.to: Primarily single-threaded and synchronous; parallel or async execution requires explicit libraries like 'future' or 'parallel'.

diffs.from: Focused on string manipulation, basic arrays, math functions, and networking interfaces.
diffs.to: Deeply focused on statistical modeling, matrix math, linear regression, and data visualization primitives.

examples.title: Vectorized Math Operations
examples.description: Replacing explicit JavaScript loops or map arrays with native, high-performance R vector operations.

examples.title: Filtering Table-like Structures
examples.description: Filtering an array of objects in JS vs. filtering a native Data Frame in R.
examples.toCode:
# Using native data frame subsetting
data <- data.frame(name = c('A', 'B'), value = c(10, 25))
filtered <- subset(data, value > 15)

tips.title: Beware of 1-Based Indexing
tips.body: R arrays and vectors start indexing at 1, unlike JavaScript's 0-based standard. Off-by-one errors are common during migration.

tips.title: Avoid Loops, Prefer Vectorization
tips.body: Do not write 'for' loops for transformations. R performs calculations across entire arrays natively and much faster using vector math.

tips.title: Use the Arrow Assignment Operator
tips.body: While '=' works in many contexts, idiomatic R utilizes '<-' for variable assignment to maintain clear scoping boundaries.

tips.title: Adopt Tidyverse for Data Pipelines
tips.body: For JS developers familiar with method chaining (.filter().map()), the Tidyverse package ecosystem in R offers equivalent '%>%' pipes.

faqs.question: Is R suitable for building production web servers?
faqs.answer: While R has frameworks like 'Plumber' for APIs and 'Shiny' for dashboards, it is built for analytics rather than serving highly concurrent web traffic.

faqs.question: How are missing values represented in R vs JS?
faqs.answer: JavaScript uses 'null' or 'undefined'. R uses specialized statistical types: 'NA' for missing values, 'NaN' for undefined math, and 'Inf' for infinity.

faqs.question: Can I invoke R scripts from a Node.js backend?
faqs.answer: Yes, you can execute R scripts via shell execution processes using Node's 'child_process' module, passing data via JSON or CSV files.
